title: Bidirectional Context for Names
concept: BERT looks both directions to classify ambiguous names (e.g. distinguishing 'Washington' as a person vs a state based on surrounding verbs).

exercises.prompt: If an AI only sees the single word 'Paris' word 'Paris', how does it know if does it know if "Paris", how does it classify it?

options.text: It could be a city or a person depending on the context.

exercises.explain: Without surrounding words (context), it's impossible to tell if 'Paris' refers to the city in France or a person's name.

cards.title: Who or Where?
cards.body: If I just say the word "Washington", are you thinking of a famous president, a state on the West Coast, or the capital city? Without context, it's impossible to know. AI has the exact same problem!

cards.title: What is Bidirectional Context?
cards.body: Bidirectional context means an AI looks at the words before AND after a specific word at the same time. This helps it understand exactly what a name refers to, even when the word itself has multiple meanings.

cards.title: The Two-Way Window
cards.body: Imagine you are trying to guess a word in a sentence with blurry glass on both sides. Traditional AI could only wipe the glass to the left to see previous words. BERT wipes the glass on both the left AND the right at the same time, giving you the full picture to name the mystery word!

cards.title: Spot the Clues
cards.body:
Look at these two sentences:
1. "Washington has beautiful forests." -> 'forests' tells us Washington is a place.
2. "Washington gave a famous speech." -> 'gave a speech' tells us Washington is a person.
BERT uses these surrounding words to easily label the name!

cards.title: Myth: One Direction is Best
cards.body: Many believe AI needs to read a sentence start-to-finish like a human to understand it. But reading in both directions at once actually makes it much easier for AI to quickly tag names and places correctly!

cards.title: One-Way vs. Two-Way Reading
cards.body: Let's compare how unidirectional (one-way) and bidirectional (two-way) AI models process a name like 'Paris'.

left.text: Reads left-to-right. Misses clues that come after the name until it's too late.

right.text: Reads left and right together. Knows 'Paris' is a person because it sees the verb 'wrote' next to it.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Georgia ruled her kingdom with kindness,' how does BERT know Georgia is a person?

options.text: It only looks at the word 'Georgia' itself.
It looks ahead to the word 'ruled' to realize it's a person.
It translates the sentence into another language.

exercises.explain: The word 'ruled' is a verb that comes after the name, giving a huge clue that 'Georgia' is a queen (a person) rather than the state or country.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A bidirectional AI only looks at the words that come before a name to understand it.
exercises.explain: Bidirectional models look at the words before and after the target word, not just the ones that come before.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete how BERT reads sentences:
exercises.sentence: To classify names correctly, BERT uses [blank] context to look left and right.
exercises.explain: By looking both ways (bidirectional), BERT has all the context it needs to figure out what a word means.

exercises.prompt: Which statement about BERT's context processing is FALSE?

options.text: BERT looks at words on both sides of a name to classify it.
Verbs like 'flew to' help BERT identify a destination name.
BERT only looks at the words before a name to guess its meaning.

exercises.explain: The third statement is incorrect because BERT looks both left and right simultaneously to understand the word, not just forward.

exercises.prompt: An AI is trying to figure out if 'Washington' is a person or a state in the sentence: 'Washington signed the treaty in 1789.' How does BERT's bidirectional context solve this?

options.text: It only reads the sentence from right to left.
It reads words both before and after the name to find clues.
It memorizes a list of every person in history.

exercises.explain: BERT reads context clues from both directions (before and after the name) to determine if 'Washington' is a person or a place. If it only read left-to-right, it would miss critical clues that come after the name!
